Psalm 97 strips away every false comfort about who rules the cosmos. The psalmist does not invite us into a gentle meditation on divine care. He presents us with a God whose presence terrifies the natural world itself—mountains liquefying like wax, lightning splitting the darkness, fire consuming His enemies. This is a throne established not on sentiment but on righteousness and justice. The clouds surrounding Him are not poetic softness; they are the hiddenness of absolute power, a reminder that we cannot see or predict or control what the Judge of all the earth decides. When the text declares "the LORD reigns," it is not announcing good news for those who live however they choose. It is announcing judgment for those who have exchanged the truth of God for idols, who have bowed before carved images and false gods. The shame that overtakes them is not incidental—it is the fitting response to a world whose true Ruler has been exposed.

Yet here is where the psalm pivots with shattering clarity: Zion hears this and rejoices. The towns of Judah exult. Not because God's judgment falls on distant enemies alone, but because His people, His covenant community, stands on the other side of His wrath. They have not worshiped idols. They have not boasted in false gods. And because they have clung to the Lord rather than traded Him for counterfeits, they experience what the wicked cannot: the preservation of their souls, deliverance from the wicked, light shining on them, gladness overflowing. The same God whose presence melts mountains in terror becomes the Guardian of His saints. This is not contradiction—it is the full logic of a righteous God.

What the psalmist is asking of you cannot be separated from what he reveals about God's reign. To "hate evil" and to "love the LORD" are not two separate commands; they are one. You cannot truly love a God who is righteous and just while tolerating what His character abhors. The idols that surround you today may not be carved wood or stone, but they are no less real—the idols of comfort, reputation, security that ask you to compromise, to bend, to serve yourself first. The psalmist calls you to see them for what they are: shameful counterfeits before a God who reigns with holy power. And He calls you to the only safe place: among His righteous ones, those who have learned that real joy, real light, real gladness come not from getting what the world promises but from standing on the side of the God whose throne is established in righteousness.

Rejoice in the LORD—not because life becomes easy, but because you have encountered the One who actually rules, whose judgments are true, and whose wrath against evil means protection for you. His glory is proclaimed by the heavens and seen by all peoples. You cannot hide from it or ignore it. The only question is whether you will stand against Him or with Him. The psalm calls you to the latter: to praise His holy name, to hate evil, to let your soul be preserved in the hands of the Most High. Everything else is shame.
